title: Of the Outrages committed by the Pagans against the Christians.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He moreover interdicted such as would not abjure Christianity, and offer sacrifice to idols, from holding any office at court: nor would he allow Christians to be governors of provinces; ‘for,’ said he, ‘their law forbids them to use the sword against offenders worthy of capital punishment.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He also induced many to sacrifice, partly by flatteries, and partly by gifts.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Immediately, as if tried in a furnace, it at once became evident to all, who were the real Christians, and who were merely nominal ones.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such as were Christians in integrity of heart, very readily resigned their commission, choosing to endure anything rather than deny Christ.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Of this number were Jovian, Valentinian, and Valens, each of whom afterwards became emperor.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But others of unsound principles, who preferred the riches and honor of this world to the true felicity, sacrificed without hesitation.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Of these was Ecebolius, a sophist of Constantinople who, accommodating himself to the dispositions of the emperors, pretended in the reign of Constantius to be an ardent Christian; while in Julian’s time he appeared an equally vigorous pagan: and after Julian’s death, he again made a profession of Christianity.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For he prostrated himself before the church doors, and called out, ‘Trample on me, for I am as salt that has lost its savor.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Of so fickle and inconstant a character was this person, throughout the whole period of his history.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: About this time the emperor wishing to make reprisals on the Persians, for the frequent incursions they had made on the Roman territories in the reign of Constantius, marched with great expedition through Asia into the East.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: But as he well knew what a train of calamities attend a war, and what immense resources are needful to carry it on successfully and that without it cannot be carried on, he craftily devised a plan for collecting money by extorting it from the Christians.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: On all those who refused to sacrifice he imposed a heavy fine, which was exacted with great rigor from such as were true Christians, every one being compelled to pay in proportion to what he possessed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: By these unjust means the emperor soon amassed immense wealth; for this law was put in execution, both where Julian was personally present, and where he was not.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The pagans at the same time assailed the Christians; and there was a great concourse of those who styled themselves ‘philosophers.’

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: They then proceeded to institute certain abominable mysteries; and sacrificing pure children both male and female, they inspected their entrails, and even tasted their flesh.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: These infamous rites were practiced in other cities, but more particularly at Athens and Alexandria; in which latter place, a calumnious accusation was made against Athanasius the bishop, the emperor being assured that he was intent on desolating not that city only, but all Egypt, and that nothing but his expulsion out of the country could save it.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The governor of Alexandria was therefore instructed by an imperial edict to apprehend him.
